expanded_description: Cytokinesis is the physical process of cell division that splits the cytoplasm of a parent cell into two daughter cells following nuclear division. This section details the distinct mechanisms of cytokinesis in animal and plant cells, the molecular machinery involved, and how this process ensures each daughter cell receives adequate cytoplasmic components.

core_concept.principle: Cytokinesis physically partitions the cytoplasm and organelles to produce two independent, functional daughter cells after nuclear division is complete.
core_concept.explanation: While mitosis divides the nucleus, cytokinesis divides the cytoplasm. In animal cells, a contractile ring of actin and myosin pinches the cell in two. In plant cells, a cell plate forms from vesicles to build a new cell wall between daughter nuclei. Both mechanisms ensure each daughter cell inherits essential organelles, proteins, and membrane systems.

image.alt: Diagram comparing cytokinesis in animal and plant cells
image.caption: Figure 7.5: Animal cells use a cleavage furrow; plant cells build a cell plate to divide the cytoplasm.

sections.introduction: Cytokinesis begins during late anaphase or telophase and completes cell division by physically separating the cytoplasm. Although coordinated with mitosis, cytokinesis employs distinct molecular machinery and varies significantly between animal and plant cells due to structural differences like the presence of a cell wall.

key_concept.definition: Cytokinesis is the process of cytoplasmic division that produces two separate daughter cells following nuclear division.
key_concept.context: Occurs in all eukaryotic cells; mechanisms differ between animal cells (cleavage furrow) and plant cells (cell plate formation) due to presence/absence of cell walls.

image.alt: Timeline showing cytokinesis overlapping with telophase
image.caption: Cytokinesis typically begins in late anaphase/telophase and completes after nuclear reformation.

classifications.value: Cleavage furrow mechanism using an actin-myosin contractile ring
classifications.behavior: Contractile ring forms at cell equator, contracts to pinch plasma membrane inward, and ultimately severs the connection between daughter cells

examples.explanation: Actin filaments and myosin II motor proteins assemble into a ring at the cell equator, positioned by signals from the mitotic spindle

image.alt: Actin-myosin ring forming at cell equator
image.caption: The contractile ring assembles at the future site of cell division, guided by spindle-derived signals.

examples.explanation: Myosin motor activity slides actin filaments, generating force that constricts the ring and pulls the plasma membrane inward

image.caption: Actin-myosin contraction drives cleavage furrow ingression to separate daughter cells.

classifications.value: Cell plate formation mechanism using vesicle fusion and new cell wall synthesis
classifications.behavior: Golgi-derived vesicles carrying cell wall materials are transported along phragmoplast microtubules to the cell center, where they fuse to form a growing cell plate that expands to fuse with the parental cell wall

examples.explanation: Microtubules and actin filaments reorganize after anaphase to form the phragmoplast, a scaffold that guides vesicles to the division plane

image.caption: The phragmoplast directs vesicle traffic to the growing cell plate in plant cells.

examples.explanation: Fused vesicles deliver cellulose synthases and matrix polysaccharides; the cell plate matures into a functional cell wall separating daughter cells

image.alt: Cell plate expanding and maturing into cell wall
image.caption: The cell plate expands outward and matures into a new cell wall between daughter cells.

principles.statement: Cytokinesis must occur precisely at the cell equator to ensure equal distribution of cytoplasmic components to daughter cells.
principles.application: Prevents production of unequal or anucleate daughter cells; ensures each inherits essential organelles and molecular machinery.
principles.example: In animal cells, the spindle midzone signals the position for contractile ring assembly; mispositioning can produce one large and one tiny daughter cell.
principles.analogy: Like cutting a cake exactly in half—precision ensures both pieces are equal and complete.

image.caption: Accurate positioning of the division plane ensures equal cytoplasmic inheritance.

principles.statement: Cytokinesis requires extensive membrane addition, fusion, and fission to separate daughter cells while maintaining membrane integrity.
principles.application: Ensures each daughter cell has a complete, functional plasma membrane; prevents leakage of cytoplasmic contents.
principles.example: In animal cells, membrane vesicles are added to the furrow region to accommodate surface area changes during constriction.
principles.analogy: Like resealing two balloons after pinching them apart—new membrane material is needed to close both ends.

image.caption: Membrane addition and fusion events support successful cell separation.

components.definition: Cytokinesis involves structural and molecular components that generate force, position the division plane, and remodel membranes.
components.interpretation: Typical constriction rates range from 0.5–2 μm/min in animal cells; plant cell plate expansion occurs at similar rates but follows a different geometric pattern (center-outward vs. periphery-inward).

image.caption: Major components include actin, myosin II, Rho GTPase, septins, and vesicle trafficking machinery.

categories.description: The actin-myosin contractile ring generates the force for cleavage furrow ingression
categories.detailed_mechanism: Actin filaments provide structural scaffold; myosin II motor proteins slide actin filaments to generate contractile force; RhoA GTPase regulates ring assembly and stability

examples.explanation: RhoA GTPase is activated at the equatorial cortex by spindle-derived signals; recruits formins to nucleate actin and ROCK to activate myosin

image.alt: RhoA signaling pathway in contractile ring assembly
image.caption: RhoA activation coordinates actin nucleation and myosin activation at the division plane.

examples.explanation: Phosphorylated myosin II heads bind actin and undergo power strokes, sliding filaments past each other to constrict the ring

image.alt: Myosin II motor activity generating contractile force
image.caption: Myosin II motor activity converts chemical energy (ATP) into mechanical force for furrow ingression.

image.alt: Detailed structure of the contractile ring
image.caption: The contractile ring is a dynamic structure requiring coordinated assembly of multiple protein components.

categories.description: Vesicle trafficking and fusion machinery builds the new cell wall between daughter cells
categories.detailed_mechanism: Golgi-derived vesicles carrying cellulose, hemicellulose, and pectin are transported along phragmoplast microtubules; SNARE proteins mediate vesicle fusion; callose is initially deposited then replaced by cellulose

examples.explanation: Exocyst complex tethers vesicles to the division plane; SNARE proteins (e.g., KNOLLE) mediate membrane fusion to expand the cell plate

image.alt: Vesicle fusion events during cell plate formation
image.caption: Coordinated vesicle fusion expands the cell plate outward from the cell center.

examples.explanation: Initial callose-rich matrix is gradually replaced by cellulose microfibrils; membrane proteins are sorted to establish distinct daughter cell plasma membranes

image.alt: Cell plate maturing into functional cell wall
image.caption: Cell plate maturation establishes a functional barrier between daughter cells.

image.alt: Molecular components of cell plate formation
image.caption: Cell plate formation requires coordinated vesicle trafficking, membrane fusion, and cell wall synthesis.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Time for Cytokinesis Completion
worked_examples.problem: If a cleavage furrow constricts at an average rate of 1.2 μm/min and the initial cell diameter at the equator is 24 μm, how long will cytokinesis take to complete (assuming symmetric constriction from both sides)?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify given values: constriction rate = 1.2 μm/min, initial diameter = 24 μm.
Since constriction occurs symmetrically from both sides, each side must close half the diameter: $d_{\text{per side}} = \frac{24 \text{ μm}}{2} = 12 \text{ μm}$.
Calculate time using $t = \frac{d}{v}$ for one side (both sides proceed simultaneously).

worked_examples.final_answer: Cytokinesis will take approximately 10 minutes to complete.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Symmetric constriction means each side of the furrow closes half the initial diameter; time depends on constriction rate and distance.

image.caption: Symmetric constriction reduces the distance each side must travel to complete division.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Cytokinesis Stage from Microscopy
worked_examples.problem: A plant cell observed under a microscope shows a growing disk-like structure at the cell center, with vesicles fusing at its edges and microtubules radiating outward from the structure. Which stage of cytokinesis is this?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify key features: disk-like structure at center, vesicle fusion at edges, radiating microtubules.
Recall that plant cells form a cell plate during cytokinesis; the phragmoplast (radiating microtubules) guides vesicles to the growing plate.
The description matches active cell plate expansion, which occurs during mid-cytokinesis before fusion with the parental wall.

worked_examples.final_answer: The cell is in the cell plate expansion stage of cytokinesis.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Plant cytokinesis is characterized by phragmoplast-guided cell plate formation, distinct from animal cell cleavage furrows.

image.alt: Micrograph of plant cell during cell plate formation
image.caption: Active cell plate expansion shows vesicle fusion and phragmoplast microtubules.

components.composition: Actin filaments, myosin II motors, Rho GTPase, septins, anillin, membrane addition vesicles
components.function: Generates contractile force to pinch plasma membrane and separate daughter cells
components.key_feature: Centripetal constriction from cell periphery toward center

image.caption: The contractile ring assembles at the equatorial cortex to drive furrow ingression.

components.composition: Phragmoplast microtubules, Golgi-derived vesicles, SNARE proteins, cellulose synthases, callose
components.function: Delivers cell wall materials to build a new partition between daughter nuclei
components.key_feature: Centrifugal expansion from cell center outward to parental wall

image.alt: Plant cell phragmoplast and cell plate
image.caption: The phragmoplast guides vesicle delivery to the expanding cell plate.

function.operation: Cytokinesis physically partitions cytoplasm, organelles, and membrane systems to produce two independent, functional daughter cells.
function.energy: ATP-dependent processes include myosin motor activity (animal cells), vesicle trafficking and fusion (plant cells), and membrane remodeling in both; energy demand peaks during active constriction/expansion.
function.mechanism: Rho GTPase signaling coordinates contractile ring assembly in animal cells; phragmoplast microtubules guide vesicle trafficking in plant cells; both pathways ensure precise positioning and execution.

image.caption: Successful cytokinesis produces two viable daughter cells with complete cytoplasmic inheritance.

cycle.operation: Signals from the mitotic spindle define the equatorial plane for cytokinesis initiation
cycle.purpose: Ensures cytokinesis occurs at the correct location to equally partition cellular contents

image.caption: Spindle midzone signals position the contractile ring or phragmoplast at the cell equator.

cycle.operation: Animal cells: actin-myosin contraction; Plant cells: phragmoplast-guided vesicle fusion
cycle.purpose: Physically separates cytoplasmic compartments while maintaining membrane integrity

image.caption: Distinct mechanisms achieve the same outcome: physical separation of daughter cells.

cycle.operation: Animal cells: membrane fission at midbody; Plant cells: cell plate fusion with parental wall
cycle.purpose: Completes physical separation and establishes independent daughter cells

image.caption: Final abscission or wall fusion completes the creation of two independent cells.

process_flow.description: RhoA GTPase is activated at the equatorial cortex by spindle midzone signals; recruits formins to nucleate actin and ROCK to activate myosin II.
process_flow.mechanism: Central spindle microtubules deliver ECT2 (RhoGEF) to the equator; active RhoA-GTP promotes actin polymerization and myosin light chain phosphorylation.
process_flow.result: A dynamic contractile ring of actin and myosin II assembles at the future division site, ready for constriction.

image.caption: RhoA signaling coordinates precise assembly of the contractile ring at the cell equator.

process_flow.process: Animal Cells: Furrow Ingression and Abscission
process_flow.description: Myosin II motor activity slides actin filaments, constricting the ring; membrane is added to accommodate shape changes; final separation occurs at the midbody.
process_flow.mechanism: Myosin power strokes generate contractile force; ESCRT-III complex mediates membrane fission at the narrow intercellular bridge (midbody).
process_flow.result: Cleavage furrow ingresses completely; ESCRT-mediated abscission severs the connection, producing two separate daughter cells.

image.caption: Coordinated constriction and membrane fission complete animal cell cytokinesis.

process_flow.process: Plant Cells: Phragmoplast Assembly and Vesicle Delivery
process_flow.description: After anaphase, microtubules reorganize into the phragmoplast; Golgi-derived vesicles carrying cell wall materials are transported to the division plane.
process_flow.mechanism: Kinesin motors transport vesicles along phragmoplast microtubules; exocyst complex tethers vesicles; SNARE proteins (e.g., KNOLLE) mediate fusion.
process_flow.result: Vesicles fuse at the cell center to initiate the cell plate, which contains membrane and cell wall precursors.
applications: Delivers building materials for new wall

image.caption: The phragmoplast acts as a dynamic scaffold for targeted vesicle delivery to the division plane.

process_flow.process: Plant Cells: Cell Plate Expansion and Maturation
process_flow.description: The cell plate expands outward toward the parental cell wall; callose is initially deposited then replaced by cellulose; membrane proteins are sorted.
process_flow.mechanism: Continued vesicle fusion expands the plate; cellulose synthase complexes are activated; callose is enzymatically replaced by cellulose microfibrils.
process_flow.result: Cell plate fuses with parental plasma membrane and cell wall; two daughter cells are separated by a functional new cell wall.
applications: Enables independent growth of daughter cells

image.caption: Cell plate expansion completes plant cytokinesis by building a new cell wall between daughter cells.

process_types.energy_profile: ATP-dependent motor activity and membrane trafficking; direction: equator → separation
process_types.function: Adaptations ensure successful division in cells with unique structural constraints

examples.explanation: Division plane is positioned off-center to produce one large stem cell and one smaller differentiating daughter; requires precise regulation of contractile ring positioning

image.caption: Asymmetric cytokinesis enables differential inheritance of cell fate determinants.

examples.explanation: Extensive phragmoplast expansion and vesicle delivery are required to traverse large cell volumes; microtubule dynamics adapt to cell size

image.alt: Cell plate expansion in large plant cell
image.caption: Plant cells scale phragmoplast dynamics to accommodate large cell dimensions.
Cytokinesis mechanisms adapt to cell type-specific requirements while maintaining core functions.

sections.definition: Cytokinesis represents the terminal execution phase of cell division that physically separates daughter cells.
sections.purpose: To partition cytoplasm, organelles, and membrane systems into two independent, functional daughter cells following nuclear division, ensuring each inherits the components necessary for survival and function.

activation.definition: Cytokinesis initiation is triggered by signals from the anaphase spindle midzone that activate Rho GTPase (animal cells) or reorganize microtubules into the phragmoplast (plant cells).
activation.effect: Ensures cytokinesis begins only after chromosome segregation is complete, preventing division errors such as chromosome cutting or unequal inheritance.

image.caption: Spindle midzone signals coordinate cytokinesis initiation with completion of chromosome segregation.

key_properties.explanation: Cytokinesis is temporally coordinated with telophase but employs distinct molecular machinery; failure to coordinate can produce binucleate or anucleate cells.

image.alt: Temporal coordination of mitosis and cytokinesis
image.caption: Precise timing ensures nuclear division completes before cytoplasmic separation begins.

key_properties.explanation: Cytokinesis requires balanced membrane addition and removal to maintain surface area-to-volume ratios; excess or deficient membrane remodeling compromises daughter cell viability.

image.caption: Controlled membrane trafficking maintains integrity during cell separation.

key_properties.explanation: Cytokinesis must distribute essential organelles (mitochondria, ER, Golgi) to both daughters; passive diffusion and active transport mechanisms ensure adequate inheritance.

image.caption: Organelles are partitioned to ensure both daughter cells receive functional cellular machinery.

key_properties.explanation: Final membrane fission (abscission) is delayed if chromatin remains in the intercellular bridge, preventing DNA damage; this checkpoint operates independently of the spindle assembly checkpoint.

image.caption: The abscission checkpoint prevents final separation if chromosomes are trapped in the division plane.

summary: Cytokinesis is the physical process that divides the cytoplasm to produce two separate daughter cells following nuclear division. In animal cells, an actin-myosin contractile ring assembles at the equator and constricts to form a cleavage furrow, with final separation mediated by ESCRT-dependent abscission. In plant cells, Golgi-derived vesicles are guided by the phragmoplast to the cell center, where they fuse to form a cell plate that expands outward and matures into a new cell wall. Both mechanisms ensure precise positioning at the cell equator, accurate partitioning of cytoplasmic components, and maintenance of membrane integrity. Cytokinesis is coordinated with mitosis but employs distinct molecular machinery; checkpoints prevent final separation if chromosomes remain in the division plane. Successful cytokinesis completes cell division by producing two independent, functional daughter cells ready to enter G₁ phase. Molecular regulation of cytokinesis timing and positioning is addressed in Section 7.6.
